name: Louis Jordan
description: American jazz, blues and rhythm and blues musician, songwriter and bandleader (1908–1975)
abstract: Louis Thomas Jordan (July 8, 1908 – February 4, 1975) was an American jazz, blues and jump blues saxophonist, vocalist, songwriter and bandleader who was popular from the late 1930s to the early 1950s. Known as "the King of the Jukebox", he earned his highest profile towards the end of the swing era.

Stone Cold Dead in the Market (He Had It Coming)

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Davis
about_quotes.evidence: listening to records, Duke Ellington, Count Basie, Louis Jordan, all the big bands during the time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Charles Davis interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Jonah Jones
about_quotes.evidence: the piano player was named Bill Austin. He used to play with Louis Jordan.

about_quotes.speaker: Al Hall
about_quotes.evidence: I remember working the first dates bass with Louis Jordan's band.

about_quotes.speaker: Leonard Phillips
about_quotes.evidence: Coridge asked the guy who was managing the band, named Vandenberg... He told me, 'Phil, how would you like to make about a month with Louis Jordan?' I said, 'Fine.' ... During that time, you played with Louis Jordan. Yes.

about_quotes.speaker: George "Tex" Stephens
about_quotes.evidence: I can remember doing a show back in the 1970s with Louis Jordan before he passed. Wallace Davenport, myself and a few others, we were at the Marriott Hotel.

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: I was writing for Louis Jordan. ... I did write one for Louis Jordan called I’ll Die Happy ... I wrote that for Louis Jordan. He recorded that on Decca.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
